perturbation.original_text: V. No Right to Confidential Information . A. The Receiving Party hereby agrees and acknowledges that no license, either express or implied, is hereby granted to the Receiving Party by the other Party to use any of the Confidential Information. B. The Receiving Party further agrees that all inventions, improvements, copyrightable works and designs relating to machines, methods, compositions, or products of the other Party directly resulting from or relating to the Confidential Information and the right to market, use, license and franchise the Confidential Information or the ideas, concepts, methods or practices embodied therein shall be the exclusive property of the other Party, and the Receiving Party has no right or title thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: V. No Right to Protected Material. A. The Receiving Party hereby agrees and acknowledges that no license, either express or implied, is hereby granted to the Receiving Party by the other Party to use any of the Protected Material. B. The Receiving Party further agrees that all inventions, improvements, copyrightable works and designs relating to machines, methods, compositions, or products of the other Party directly resulting from or relating to the Protected Material and the right to market, use, license and franchise the Protected Material or the ideas, concepts, methods or practices embodied therein shall be the exclusive property of the other Party, and the Receiving Party has no right or title thereto.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Material' limits the scope of rights. 'Protected Material' is not defined anywhere in the contract leading to uncertainty about its meaning, in contrast with the original 'Confidential Information' defined in Section I. The original text enforces that the receiving party cannot use 'Confidential Information' for business purposes, 'Protected Material' on the other hand, could be misinterpreted and potentially affect the business between parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: I. "Confidential Information ". For purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information shall mean all strategic and development plans, financial condition, business plans, co-developer identities, data, business records, customer lists, project records, market reports, employee lists and business manuals, policies and procedures, information relating to processes, technologies or theory and all other information which may be disclosed by one Party or to which the other Party may be provided access by the disclosing Party or others in accordance with this Agreement, or which is generated as a result of , incidental to or in connection with the Business Purposes, which is not generally available to the public.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Confidential Information', which is clearly defined in Section I. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Protected Material', which is undefined. This creates a contradiction because Section I defines 'Confidential Information' and the altered Section V now refers to 'Protected Material' without a definition, thus making its meaning ambiguous compared to the defined term, leading to misinterpretation and uncertainty regarding the rights and obligations of the parties.

perturbation.original_text: VI. No Warranty . The Disclosing Party has not made and will not make any representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of its Confidential Information or of any other information provided to the Receiving Party, and the Receiving Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have no liability resulting from the use of the Confidential Information or such other information.
perturbation.changed_text: VI. No Warranty. The Disclosing Party has not made and will not make any representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of its Sensitive Information or of any other information provided to the Receiving Party, and the Receiving Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have no liability resulting from the use of the Sensitive Information or such other information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Sensitive Information' in the 'No Warranty' clause creates ambiguity because the term 'Sensitive Information' is never defined in the contract. The lack of a clear definition for 'Sensitive Information' creates uncertainty about what the Disclosing Party is not warranting, especially compared to the original intent and definition of 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: I. "Confidential Information ". For purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information shall mean all strategic and development plans, financial condition, business plans, co-developer identities, data, business records, customer lists, project records, market reports, employee lists and business manuals, policies and procedures, information relating to processes, technologies or theory and all other information which may be disclosed by one Party or to which the other Party may be provided access by the disclosing Party or others in accordance with this Agreement, or which is generated as a result of , incidental to or in connection with the Business Purposes, which is not generally available to the public.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Information' creates a strong in-text contradiction. The original text defines 'Confidential Information' comprehensively. By replacing it with 'Sensitive Information', which is undefined, the 'No Warranty' clause becomes ambiguous, directly conflicting with the defined scope and clarity of 'Confidential Information' in Section I. This introduces uncertainty about what information is not warranted, making the agreement internally inconsistent and potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: IX. Losses . The Receiving Party agrees to indemnify the other Party against any and all losses, damages, claims, or expenses incurred or suffered by the other Party as a result of the Receiving Party's breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: IX. Losses . The Receiving Party agrees to indemnify the other Party against any and all losses, damages, claims, or expenses incurred or suffered by the other Party as a result of the Receiving Party's infringement of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'breach' to 'infringement' alters the standard for triggering indemnification. 'Breach' generally refers to any violation of the agreement's terms, while 'infringement' typically refers to a violation of intellectual property rights. It will create confusion as a lawyer will argue if the 'Confidential Information' is also covered in the infringement. There's an in-text contradiction, as now 'Infringement' may not cover the protection of 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: II. Non-disclosure Obligations . The Receiving Party promises and agrees to receive and hold the Confidential Information in confidence. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, the Receiving Party further promises and agrees: A. to protect and safeguard the Confidential Information against unauthorized use, publication or disclosure; B. not to use any of the Confidential Information except for the Business Purposes. C. not to, directly or indirectly, in any way, reveal, report, publish, disclose, transfer or otherwise use any of the Confidential Information except as specifically authorized by the Disclosing Party in accordance with this Non-Disclosure Agreement. D. not to use any Confidential Information to unfairly compete or obtain unfair advantage vis-a-vis Disclosing Party in any commercial activity which may be comparable to the commercial activity contemplated by the parties in connection with the Business Purposes. E. to restrict access to the Confidential Information to those of its officers, directors, and employees who clearly need such access to carry out the Business Purposes. F. to advise each of the persons to whom it provides access to any of the Confidential Information, that such persons are strictly prohibited from making any use, publishing or otherwise disclosing to others, or permitting others to use for their benefit or to the detriment of the Disclosing Party, any of the Confidential Information, and, upon Request of the Disclosing Party, to provide the Disclosing Party with a copy of a written agreement to that effect signed by such persons. G. to comply with any other reasonable security measures requested in writing by the Disclosing Party. H. To refrain from directly contacting or communicating by whatsoever means to the Source(s) of Information without written consent of the Disclosing Party. I. To undertake not to disclose any names and their particulars to third parties without the written consent by the Disclosing party.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'breach' to define when indemnification is triggered, which encompasses any violation of the agreement. The changed text narrows this to 'infringement,' which typically relates to intellectual property. The contradicted text outlines non-disclosure obligations related to 'Confidential Information'. By changing 'breach' to 'infringement', the receiving party may no longer be liable for losses resulting from a breach of the non-disclosure obligations concerning confidential information, as 'infringement' might not cover this, thus contradicting the broad protection intended by Section II. This misalignment of terminology creates a strong in-text contradiction because the scope of indemnification is significantly reduced, potentially excluding breaches of confidentiality which were originally covered.

perturbation.original_text: XIV. Remedies . The Receiving Party understands and acknowledges that any disclosure or misappropriation of any of the Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement may cause the Disclosing Party irreparable harm, the amount of which may be difficult to ascertain and, therefore, agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for an order restraining any such further disclosure or misappropriation and for such other relief as the Disclosing Party shall deem appropriate.
perturbation.changed_text: XIV. Remedies. The Receiving Party understands and acknowledges that any disclosure or misappropriation of any of the Trade Secrets in violation of this Agreement may cause the Disclosing Party irreparable harm, the amount of which may be difficult to ascertain and, therefore, agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for an order restraining any such further disclosure or misappropriation and for such other relief as the Disclosing Party shall deem appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Trade Secrets' alters the scope of the remedies available. 'Trade Secrets' have a specific legal definition that may not encompass all information originally intended to be covered by 'Confidential Information'. This discrepancy weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to seek remedies for the disclosure of information that does not qualify as a 'Trade Secret' but was intended to be protected. It contradicts the original intention to protect all forms of 'Confidential Information' and now only protects a sub-set of 'Trade Secrets'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: I. "Confidential Information ". For purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information shall mean all strategic and development plans, financial condition, business plans, co-developer identities, data, business records, customer lists, project records, market reports, employee lists and business manuals, policies and procedures, information relating to processes, technologies or theory and all other information which may be disclosed by one Party or to which the other Party may be provided access by the disclosing Party or others in accordance with this Agreement, or which is generated as a result of , incidental to or in connection with the Business Purposes, which is not generally available to the public.
perturbation.justification: The original text protected 'Confidential Information' but was changed to 'Trade Secrets'. The 'Contradicted Text' provides a broad definition of 'Confidential Information' which includes 'all strategic and development plans, financial condition, business plans, co-developer identities, data, business records, customer lists, project records, market reports, employee lists and business manuals, policies and procedures, information relating to processes, technologies or theory and all other information which may be disclosed'. By changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Trade Secrets', the change creates a contradiction because 'Trade Secrets' have a specific legal definition that may not encompass all information originally intended to be covered by 'Confidential Information' as defined in section I. This narrows the scope of protection and the available remedies, directly contradicting the original intent to protect all forms of 'Confidential Information'.
